skill.name: doc-coauthoring
skill.description: Structured workflow for collaborative documentation, proposals, technical specs, and decision docs with context gathering, iterative drafting, and reader testing.
skill.summary: Coauthor documentation through guided context gathering, section drafting, refinement, and reader testing.
skill.author: Azeem-2

security_audit.summary: Static high-severity cryptography findings are false positives from ordinary prose, and external-command findings are tool-name or file-writing instructions rather than shell execution. The skill has moderate operational risk because it guides agents to read shared documents or channels through authorized integrations and to create or edit documentation files. No prompt injection attempt, malicious exfiltration, or unauthorized network behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: User-Directed Connector and Shared Document Access
medium_findings.description: The workflow tells the agent to use available integrations to read shared documents, team channels, and related context. This is legitimate for coauthoring, but it can expose sensitive workspace content if users grant broad access without checking scope.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly discuss reading shared documents and using integrations. The workflow generally requires user context or confirmation, so the risk is moderate rather than high.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem and Artifact Modification Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to create document files or artifacts and edit them with targeted replacements. This is expected behavior for a documentation skill, but users should restrict it to intended project documents.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly instruct file or artifact creation and edit operations. There is no evidence of destructive or hidden file access.

low_findings.title: Ruby Shell Backtick Detection Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged inline command-style text such as create_file and str_replace as Ruby shell backticks. These are documented tool names or markdown examples, not executable Ruby code or shell command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant text is inside Markdown instructions, not Ruby source code. No interpolation, shell invocation, or executable script context is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detection Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography matches occur in normal documentation prose. No cryptographic algorithm, hash function, cipher configuration, or security-sensitive implementation appears on these lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found only natural language workflow text at each cited location. There is no code or cryptographic behavior to exploit.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is a Benign Manual Testing Link
low_findings.description: The only hardcoded URL points users to https://claude.ai for manual reader testing. It does not send data automatically, and no hidden external endpoint is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is shown as a user-facing instruction to open Claude manually. There is no fetch call, webhook, or automated network request.

low_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance Detection Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The static finding at the info-dump instruction is ordinary writing guidance. It does not request port scanning, host discovery, DNS enumeration, or other reconnaissance activity.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line asks users to provide context in a stream-of-consciousness format. No network target or reconnaissance command appears there.

content.value_statement: Complex documents fail when context, audience needs, and review feedback stay scattered. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured coauthoring workflow for drafting, refining, and testing documents.
actual_capabilities: Guides context gathering for audience, document type, constraints, and desired impact.
Builds a document outline with section placeholders before drafting details.
Brainstorms section content and helps users choose what to keep or remove.
Drafts and refines sections through targeted edits instead of full rewrites.
Tests finished documents with sub-agents or manual Claude reader prompts.
Checks complete drafts for flow, redundancy, assumptions, and missing context.
limitations: Requires user-provided context or explicitly authorized connectors.
Does not independently verify facts, links, or technical claims.
Can be slower than freeform drafting for small documents.
File and artifact editing depends on available tool access.

use_cases.title: Draft a Technical Spec
use_cases.description: Turn scattered design context into a structured spec with clear trade-offs, constraints, and implementation details.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Decision Document
use_cases.description: Capture goals, alternatives, stakeholder concerns, and final recommendations in a format readers can evaluate quickly.

use_cases.title: Improve Internal Documentation
use_cases.description: Refine existing docs by finding unclear assumptions, missing context, redundant sections, and reader confusion.

prompt_templates.title: Start a New Document
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me draft a technical document. Start by asking for the document type, audience, goal, template, and constraints.

prompt_templates.title: Turn Notes Into a Draft
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this context dump to propose a document structure, identify missing information, and draft the first section after I approve the outline.

prompt_templates.title: Refine an Existing Section
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this section for clarity, redundancy, unsupported claims, and reader assumptions. Suggest targeted edits before rewriting anything.

prompt_templates.title: Run Reader Testing
prompt_templates.prompt: Test this document as a new reader. Predict likely reader questions, identify unclear assumptions, and recommend focused fixes.

output_examples.input: I need a proposal for changing our deployment process.
output_examples.output: A guided intake covering audience, desired decision, current process, constraints, risks, and needed evidence before drafting begins.

output_examples.input: Here is a rough outline for an RFC. Help improve it.
output: A clearer section order based on the decision being made.
Questions about missing alternatives, rollout plans, and reader assumptions.
A plan to draft each section through reviewable iterations.

output_examples.input: The spec is done. Check whether readers will understand it.
output_examples.output: A reader-testing report that lists likely questions, unclear context, contradictions, and specific sections to revise.

best_practices: Share audience, goal, constraints, and source context before asking for polished prose.
Approve the outline before drafting full sections.
Use reader testing before sending important documents to stakeholders.
anti_patterns: Do not connect broad workspaces unless the document needs that context.
Do not skip fact checking for links, metrics, dates, or technical claims.
Do not ask for a full rewrite when targeted section edits are enough.

faq.question: What types of documents does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports proposals, technical specs, decision docs, RFCs, PRDs, and similar structured documents.

faq.question: Does it write the whole document at once?
faq.answer: No. It builds context first, then drafts and refines one section at a time.

faq.question: Can it use shared documents or team channels?
faq.answer: Yes, if authorized integrations are available and the user chooses to provide that context.

faq.question: Does it verify every factual claim?
faq.answer: No. Users should check facts, links, dates, and technical details before publication.

faq.question: What is reader testing?
faq.answer: Reader testing checks whether a fresh Claude conversation can understand the document without prior context.

faq.question: Is it suitable for short notes?
faq.answer: It can help, but the full workflow is most useful for substantial documents with multiple readers.
